summary_text:
S:
The patient reports pain in the left hand. He reports swelling in his ankles, primarily near the end of the day, which resolves by the next morning. He reports occasional consumption of burgers and wine. The patient reports feeling well managing his depression with counseling once a week and does not want to start medication at this time.

O:
The patient's blood pressure is elevated today, measuring 156/94. A slight three out of six systolic ejection murmur is heard. The patient reports 1-2+ pitting edema in his legs. The patient's EKG shows no evidence of coronary artery disease. The patient's creatinine is stable. The doctor performs a physical exam and notes the findings described above.

A:
The patient's hypertension is not well controlled. Depression is the patient's first problem. The patient has a past medical history significant for prior rotator cuff repair and depression.

P:
Increase norvasc to 10 mg a day. Order a lipid panel. Order an echocardiogram. Recommend decreasing salt intake. Follow up if symptoms worsen or do not improve. Schedule counseling once a week.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Follow-up of chronic problems.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Edward Butler is a 59-year-old male with a past medical history significant for depression, hypertension, and prior rotator cuff repair. He presents for a follow-up of his chronic problems.

Regarding his depression, he has been doing pretty well over the last 6 months. The patient notes that he sees a counselor once a week. He states that he has been swimming at the pool a lot this summer and fall. The patient has preferred to avoid medications to treat this.

Regarding his hypertension, he states that he has good days and bad days. He adds that he takes his Norvasc daily. The patient states that he checks his blood pressure at CVS about once weekly. He does admit to occasionally drinking wine and eating burgers.

The patient had his rotator cuff repaired about 8 months ago. He states that he is doing well. He states that he is no longer seeing a physical therapist in this center, however, he is progressing to exercises at home. The patient notes that he stretches with a yoga ball and is getting stronger.

He notes that he experiences mild swelling in his ankles, mainly near the end of the day. He states that the swelling resolves by the next morning. The patient denies nasal congestion, chest pain, or shortness of breath.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Ears, Nose, Mouth and Throat: Denies nasal congestion.
• Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain or dyspnea on exertion.
• Respiratory: Denies shortness of breath
• Musculoskeletal: Bilateral ankle swelling.
• Psychiatric: Endorses depression.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Neurological/Psychological: Appropriate mood and affect.
• Respiratory: Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
• Cardiovascular: Slight 3/6 systolic ejection murmur, stable.
• Musculoskeletal: 1-2+ pitting edema in the bilateral lower extremities.

VITALS REVIEWED

• Blood Pressure: Elevated at 156/94 mmHg.

RESULTS

Labs: Creatinine is within normal limits.
Electrocardiogram is normal. No evidence of coronary artery disease.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Edward Butler is a 59-year-old male with a past medical history significant for depression, hypertension, and prior rotator cuff repair. He presents for a follow-up of his chronic problems.

Depression.
• Medical Reasoning: He is doing well with therapy and physical activity. He continues to decline medication for this.
• Medical Treatment: Continue current management strategies.
• Patient Education and Counseling: I encouraged him to contact me should he want to try medication.

Hypertension.
• Medical Reasoning: This is uncontrolled at this time. He is compliant with Norvasc, but admits to occasional dietary indiscretion.
• Additional Testing: We will order a lipid panel and an echocardiogram.
• Medical Treatment: Increase Norvasc to 10 mg daily.

8 months status post rotator cuff repair.
• Medical Reasoning: He is doing well postoperatively. He has progressed from formal physical therapy to solely home exercise.
• Medical Treatment: Continue with home exercise.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

sections.heading: S
sections.section_value: The patient reports pain in the left hand. He reports swelling in his ankles, primarily near the end of the day, which resolves by the next morning. He reports occasional consumption of burgers and wine. The patient reports feeling well managing his depression with counseling once a week and does not want to start medication at this time.

sections.heading: O
sections.section_value: The patient's blood pressure is elevated today, measuring 156/94. A slight three out of six systolic ejection murmur is heard. The patient reports 1-2+ pitting edema in his legs. The patient's EKG shows no evidence of coronary artery disease. The patient's creatinine is stable. sections.heading: A
sections.section_value: The patient's hypertension is not well controlled. Depression is the patient's first problem. The patient has a past medical history significant for prior rotator cuff repair and depression.

sections.heading: P
sections.section_value: Increase norvasc to 10 mg a day. Order a lipid panel. Order an echocardiogram. Recommend decreasing salt intake. Follow up if symptoms worsen or do not improve. Schedule counseling once a week.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports 1-2+ pitting edema in his legs.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Pitting edema grading (1-2+) is an objective physical exam-style description; placing it as something the patient reports blurs subjective vs. objective data and could mislead a reader about how the finding was obtained.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient's EKG shows no evidence of coronary artery disease.
section_miscategorizations.reason: While EKG is an objective test and belongs in O, stating that it shows 'no evidence of coronary artery disease' is an interpretive diagnostic conclusion rather than a raw finding; this interpretive synthesis more appropriately belongs in the assessment.

section_miscategorizations.text: Depression is the patient's first problem.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The content (prioritizing depression as a problem) is appropriate for Assessment, but the phrasing is awkward and could be misread as a historical statement; however, it does not clearly belong in another section, so this is only a minor organizational issue.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient has a past medical history significant for prior rotator cuff repair and depression.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Past medical history is typically documented in the subjective/history portion rather than in the assessment, which should focus on current diagnostic impressions and active problems.

In this note, the patient already reports receiving counseling once a week in the Subjective section; restating 'Schedule counseling once a week' in the Plan implies a new order rather than continuation of an existing therapy, which could mislead about what is changing. It would be clearer either as confirmation of continuation or omitted as a new action.
